73    for (int i = 0; i < equations.size(); i++) {
74        String dividend = equations.get(i).get(0);
75        String divisor = equations.get(i).get(1);
76        double value = values[i];
77
78        gr.putIfAbsent(dividend, new HashMap<>());
79        gr.putIfAbsent(divisor, new HashMap<>());
80
81        gr.get(dividend).put(divisor, value);
82        gr.get(divisor).put(dividend, 1.0 / value);
83    }
84
85    return gr;
86}

2-10) Most Frequent Subtree Sum

1# LC 508 Most Frequent Subtree Sum
2# V0
3# IDEA : DFS + TREE
4class Solution(object):
5    def findFrequentTreeSum(self, root):
6        """
7        ### NOTE : this trick : get sum of sub tree
8        # LC 663 Equal Tree Partition
9        """
10        def get_sum(root):
11            if not root:
12                return 0
13            s = get_sum(root.left) + root.val + get_sum(root.right)
14            res.append(s)
15            return s
16
17        if not root:
18            return []
19        res = []
20        get_sum(root)
21        counts = collections.Counter(res)
22        _max = max(counts.values())
23        return [x for x in counts if counts[x] == _max]
24
25# V0'
26# IDEA : DFS + COUNTER
27from collections import Counter
28class Solution(object):
29    def findFrequentTreeSum(self, root):
30        def helper(root, d):
31            if not root:
32                return 0
33            left = helper(root.left, d)
34            right = helper(root.right, d)
35            subtreeSum = left + right + root.val
36            d[subtreeSum] = d.get(subtreeSum, 0) + 1
37            return subtreeSum      
38        d = {}
39        helper(root, d)
40        mostFreq = 0
41        ans = []
42        print ("d = " + str(d))
43        _max_cnt = max(d.values())
44        ans = []
45        return [x for x in d if d[x] == _max_cnt]

2-11) Convert BST to Greater Tree

1# LC 538 Convert BST to Greater Tree
2# V0
3# IDEA : DFS + recursion
4#      -> NOTE : via DFS, the op will being executed in `INVERSE` order (last visit will be run first, then previous, then ...)
5#      -> e.g. node1 -> node2 -> ... nodeN
6#      ->      will run nodeN -> nodeN-1 ... node1
7class Solution(object):
8
9    def convertBST(self, root):
10        self.sum = 0
11        self.dfs(root)
12        return root
13
14    def dfs(self, node):
15        if not node: 
16            return
17        #print ("node.val = " + str(node.val))
18        self.dfs(node.right)
19        self.sum += node.val
20        node.val = self.sum
21        self.dfs(node.left)
22
23# V0'
24# NOTE : the implementation difference on cur VS self.cur
25# 1) if cur : we need to ssign output of help() func to cur
26# 2) if self.cur : no need to assign, plz check V0 as reference
27class Solution(object):
28    def convertBST(self, root):
29        def help(cur, root):
30            if not root:
31                ### NOTE : if not root, still need to return cur
32                return cur
33            ### NOTE : need to assign output of help() func to cur
34            cur = help(cur, root.right)
35            cur += root.val
36            root.val = cur
37            ### NOTE : need to assign output of help() func to cur
38            cur = help(cur, root.left)
39            ### NOTE : need to return cur
40            return cur
41
42        if not root:
43            return
44
45        cur = 0
46        help(cur, root)
47        return root

2-12) Number of Islands

1# LC 200 Number of Islands, check LC 694, 711 as well
2# V0 
3# IDEA : DFS
4class Solution(object):
5    def numIslands(self, grid):
6        def dfs(grid, item):
7            if grid[item[0]][item[1]] == "0":
8                return
9
10            ### NOTE : MAKE grid[item[0]][item[1]] = 0 -> avoid visit again
11            grid[item[0]][item[1]] = 0
12            moves = [(0,1),(0,-1),(1,0),(-1,0)]
13            for move in moves:
14                _x = item[0] + move[0]
15                _y = item[1] + move[1]
16                ### NOTE : the boundary
17                #       -> _x < l, _y < w
18                if 0 <= _x < l and 0 <= _y < w and grid[_x][_y] != 0:
19                    dfs(grid, [_x, _y])
20  
21        if not grid:
22            return 0
23        res = 0
24        l = len(grid)
25        w = len(grid[0])
26        for i in range(l):
27            for j in range(w):
28                if grid[i][j] == "1":
29                    ### NOTE : we go through every "1" in grids, and run dfs once
30                    #         -> once dfs completed, we make res += 1 in each iteration
31                    dfs(grid, [i,j])
32                    res += 1
33        return res

2-13) Max Area of Island

1# LC 695. Max Area of Island
2# V1
3# https://blog.csdn.net/fuxuemingzhu/article/details/79182435
4# IDEA : DFS 
5# * PLEASE NOTE THAT IT IS NEEDED TO GO THROUGH EVERY ELEMENT IN THE GRID 
6#   AND RUN THE DFS WITH IN THIS PROBLEM
7class Solution(object):
8    def maxAreaOfIsland(self, grid):
9        """
10        :type grid: List[List[int]]
11        :rtype: int
12        """
13        self.res = 0
14        self.island = 0
15        M, N = len(grid), len(grid[0])
16        for i in range(M):
17            for j in range(N):
18                if grid[i][j]:
19                    self.dfs(grid, i, j)
20                    self.res = max(self.res, self.island)
21                    self.island = 0
22        return self.res
23    
24    def dfs(self, grid, i, j): # ensure grid[i][j] == 1
25        M, N = len(grid), len(grid[0])
26        grid[i][j] = 0
27        self.island += 1
28        dirs = [(0, 1), (0, -1), (-1, 0), (1, 0)]
29        for d in dirs:
30            x, y = i + d[0], j + d[1]
31            if 0 <= x < M and 0 <= y < N and grid[x][y]:
32                self.dfs(grid, x, y)

2-14) Binary Tree Paths

1# LC 257. Binary Tree Paths
2# V0 
3# IDEA : DFS 
4class Solution:
5    # @param {TreeNode} root
6    # @return {string[]}
7    def binaryTreePaths(self, root):
8        res, path_list = [], []
9        self.dfs(root, path_list, res)
10        return res
11
12    def dfs(self, root, path_list, res):
13        if not root:
14            return
15        path_list.append(str(root.val))
16        if not root.left and not root.right:
17            res.append('->'.join(path_list))
18        if root.left:
19            self.dfs(root.left, path_list, res)
20        if root.right:
21            self.dfs(root.right, path_list, res)
22        path_list.pop()

2-15) Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree

1# LC 236 Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree
2# V0
3# IDEA : RECURSION + POST ORDER TRANSVERSAL
4class Solution(object):
5    def lowestCommonAncestor(self, root, p, q):
6
7        ### NOTE here
8        # if not root or find p in tree or find q in tree
9        # -> then we quit the recursion and return root
10
11        ### NOTE : we compare `p == root` and  `q == root`
12        if not root or p == root or q == root:
13            return root
14        ### NOTE here
15        #  -> not root.left, root.right, BUT left, right
16        left = self.lowestCommonAncestor(root.left, p, q)
17        right = self.lowestCommonAncestor(root.right, p, q)
18
19        ### NOTE here
20        # find q and p on the same time -> LCA is the current node (root)
21        # if left and right -> p, q MUST in left, right sub tree respectively
22
23        ### NOTE : if left and right, means this root is OK for next recursive
24        if left and right:
25            return root
26        ### NOTE here
27        # if p, q both in left sub tree or both in right sub tree
28        return left if left else right

2-16) Path Sum

1# LC 112 Path Sum
2# V0
3# IDEA : DFS 
4class Solution(object):
5    def hasPathSum(self, root, sum):
6        if not root:
7            return False
8        if not root.left and not root.right:
9            return True if sum == root.val else False
10        else:
11            return self.hasPathSum(root.left, sum-root.val) or self.hasPathSum(root.right, sum-root.val)

2-17) Path Sum II

1# LC 113 Path Sum II
2# V0
3# IDEA : DFS
4class Solution(object):
5    def pathSum(self, root, sum):
6        if not root: return []
7        res = []
8        self.dfs(root, sum, res, [root.val])
9        return res
10
11    def dfs(self, root, target, res, path):
12        if not root: return
13        if sum(path) == target and not root.left and not root.right:
14            res.append(path)
15            return
16        if root.left:
17            self.dfs(root.left, target, res, path + [root.left.val])
18        if root.right:
19            self.dfs(root.right, target, res, path + [root.right.val])

1// java
2// LC 113
3// V0
4// IDEA : DFS + backtracking
5// NOTE !!! we have res attr, so can use this.res collect result
6private List<List<Integer>> res = new ArrayList<>();
7
8public List<List<Integer>> pathSum(TreeNode root, int targetSum) {
9
10    if (root == null){
11        return this.res;
12    }
13
14    List<Integer> cur = new ArrayList<>();
15    getPath(root, cur, targetSum);
16    return this.res;
17}
18
19 private void getPath(TreeNode root, List<Integer> cur, int targetSum){
20
21    // return directly if root is null (not possible to go further, so just quit directly)
22    if (root == null){
23        return;
24    }
25
26    // NOTE !!! we add val to cache here instead of while calling method recursively ( e.g. getPath(root.left, cur, targetSum - root.val))
27    //          -> so we just need to backtrack (cancel last operation) once (e.g. cur.remove(cur.size() - 1);)
28    //          -> please check V0' for example with backtrack in recursively step
29    cur.add(root.val);
30
31    if (root.left == null && root.right == null && targetSum == root.val){
32        this.res.add(new ArrayList<>(cur));
33    }else{
34        // NOTE !!! we update targetSum here (e.g. targetSum - root.val)
35        getPath(root.left, cur, targetSum - root.val);
36        getPath(root.right, cur, targetSum - root.val);
37    }
38
39     // NOTE !!! we do backtrack here (cancel previous adding to cur)
40     cur.remove(cur.size() - 1);
41}

2-7) Clone graph

1# 133 Clone graph
2# note : there is also a BFS solution
3# V0
4# IDEA : DFS
5# NOTE :
6#  -> 1) we init node via : node_copy = Node(node.val, [])
7#  -> 2) we copy graph via dict
8class Solution(object):
9    def cloneGraph(self, node):
10        """
11        :type node: Node
12        :rtype: Node
13        """
14        node_copy = self.dfs(node, dict())
15        return node_copy
16    
17    def dfs(self, node, hashd):
18        if not node: return None
19        if node in hashd: return hashd[node]
20        node_copy = Node(node.val, [])
21        hashd[node] = node_copy
22        for n in node.neighbors:
23            n_copy = self.dfs(n, hashd)
24            if n_copy:
25                node_copy.neighbors.append(n_copy)
26        return node_copy

2-8) Sentence Similarity II

1# LC 737. Sentence Similarity II
2# NOTE : there is also union-find solution
3# V0
4# IDEA : DFS
5from collections import defaultdict
6class Solution(object):
7    def areSentencesSimilarTwo(self, sentence1, sentence2, similarPairs):
8        # helper func
9        def dfs(w1, w2, visited):
10            for j in d[w2]:
11                if w1 == w2:
12                    return True
13                elif j not in visited:
14                    visited.add(j)
15                    if dfs(w1, j, visited):
16                        return True
17            return False
18        
19        # edge case
20        if len(sentence1) != len(sentence2):
21            return False
22      
23        d = defaultdict(list)
24        for a, b in similarPairs:
25            d[a].append(b)
26            d[b].append(a)
27            
28        for i in range(len(sentence1)):
29            visited =  set([sentence2[i]])
30            if sentence1[i] != sentence2[i] and not dfs(sentence1[i],  sentence2[i], visited):
31                return False
32        return True

2-9) Concatenated Words

1# LC 472. Concatenated Words
2# V1
3# http://bookshadow.com/weblog/2016/12/18/leetcode-concatenated-words/
4# IDEA : DFS 
5class Solution(object):
6    def findAllConcatenatedWordsInADict(self, words):
7        """
8        :type words: List[str]
9        :rtype: List[str]
10        """
11        ans = []
12        self.wordSet = set(words)
13        for word in words:
14            self.wordSet.remove(word) # avoid the search process find itself (word) when search all word in words  
15            if self.search(word):
16                ans.append(word)
17            self.wordSet.add(word)    # add the word back for next search with new "word"
18        return ans
19
20    def search(self, word):
21        if word in self.wordSet:
22            return True
23        for idx in range(1, len(word)):
24            if word[:idx] in self.wordSet and self.search(word[idx:]):
25                return True
26        return False

2-10) Maximum Product of Splitted Binary Tree

1# LC 1339. Maximum Product of Splitted Binary Tree
2# V0
3# IDEA : DFS
4class Solution(object):
5    def maxProduct(self, root):
6        all_sums = []
7
8        def tree_sum(subroot):
9            if subroot is None: return 0
10            left_sum = tree_sum(subroot.left)
11            right_sum = tree_sum(subroot.right)
12            total_sum = left_sum + right_sum + subroot.val
13            all_sums.append(total_sum)
14            return total_sum
15
16        total = tree_sum(root)
17        best = 0
18        for s in all_sums:
19            best = max(best, s * (total - s))   
20        return best % (10 ** 9 + 7)

2-11) Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree

1# LC 297. Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
2# V0
3# IDRA : DFS
4class Codec:
5
6    def serialize(self, root):
7        """ Encodes a tree to a single string.
8        :type root: TreeNode
9        :rtype: str
10        """
11        def rserialize(root, string):
12            """ a recursive helper function for the serialize() function."""
13            # check base case
14            if root is None:
15                string += 'None,'
16            else:
17                string += str(root.val) + ','
18                string = rserialize(root.left, string)
19                string = rserialize(root.right, string)
20            return string
21        
22        return rserialize(root, '')    
23
24    def deserialize(self, data):
25        """Decodes your encoded data to tree.
26        :type data: str
27        :rtype: TreeNode
28        """
29        def rdeserialize(l):
30            """ a recursive helper function for deserialization."""
31            if l[0] == 'None':
32                l.pop(0)
33                return None
34                
35            root = TreeNode(l[0])
36            l.pop(0)
37            root.left = rdeserialize(l)
38            root.right = rdeserialize(l)
39            return root
40
41        data_list = data.split(',')
42        root = rdeserialize(data_list)
43        return root
44
45# V1
46# IDEA : same as LC 297
47# https://leetcode.com/problems/serialize-and-deserialize-bst/discuss/93283/Python-solution-using-BST-property
48class Codec:
49
50    def serialize(self, root):
51        vals = []
52        self._preorder(root, vals)
53        return ','.join(vals)
54        
55    def _preorder(self, node, vals):
56        if node:
57            vals.append(str(node.val))
58            self._preorder(node.left, vals)
59            self._preorder(node.right, vals)
60        
61    def deserialize(self, data):
62        vals = collections.deque(map(int, data.split(','))) if data else []
63        return self._build(vals, -float('inf'), float('inf'))
64
65    def _build(self, vals, minVal, maxVal):
66        if vals and minVal < vals[0] < maxVal:
67            val = vals.popleft()
68            root = TreeNode(val)
69            root.left = self._build(vals, minVal, val)
70            root.right = self._build(vals, val, maxVal)
71            return root
72        else:
73            return None

2-12) Serialize and Deserialize BST

1# LC 449. Serialize and Deserialize BST
2# V0
3# IDEA : BFS + queue op
4class Codec:
5    def serialize(self, root):
6        if not root:
7            return '{}'
8
9        res = [root.val]
10        q = [root]
11
12        while q:
13            new_q = []
14            for i in range(len(q)):
15                tmp = q.pop(0)
16                if tmp.left:
17                    q.append(tmp.left)
18                    res.extend( [tmp.left.val] )
19                else:
20                    res.append('#')
21                if tmp.right:
22                    q.append(tmp.right)
23                    res.extend( [tmp.right.val] )
24                else:
25                    res.append('#')
26
27        while res and res[-1] == '#':
28                    res.pop()
29
30        return '{' + ','.join(map(str, res)) + '}' 
31
32
33    def deserialize(self, data):
34        if data == '{}':
35            return
36
37        nodes = [ TreeNode(x) for x in data[1:-1].split(",") ]
38        root = nodes.pop(0)
39        p = [root]
40        while p:
41            new_p = []
42            for n in p:
43                if nodes:
44                    left_node = nodes.pop(0)
45                    if left_node.val != '#':
46                        n.left = left_node
47                        new_p.append(n.left)
48                    else:
49                        n.left = None
50                if nodes:
51                    right_node = nodes.pop(0)
52                    if right_node.val != '#':
53                        n.right = right_node
54                        new_p.append(n.right)
55                    else:
56                        n.right = None
57            p = new_p 
58             
59        return root
60
61# V1
62# IDEA : same as LC 297
63# https://leetcode.com/problems/serialize-and-deserialize-bst/discuss/93283/Python-solution-using-BST-property
64class Codec:
65
66    def serialize(self, root):
67        vals = []
68        self._preorder(root, vals)
69        return ','.join(vals)
70        
71    def _preorder(self, node, vals):
72        if node:
73            vals.append(str(node.val))
74            self._preorder(node.left, vals)
75            self._preorder(node.right, vals)
76        
77    def deserialize(self, data):
78        vals = collections.deque(map(int, data.split(','))) if data else []
79        return self._build(vals, -float('inf'), float('inf'))
80
81    def _build(self, vals, minVal, maxVal):
82        if vals and minVal < vals[0] < maxVal:
83            val = vals.popleft()
84            root = TreeNode(val)
85            root.left = self._build(vals, minVal, val)
86            root.right = self._build(vals, val, maxVal)
87            return root
88        else:
89            return None

2-12) Number of Closed Islands (2-Pass DFS)

1// java
2// LC 1254
3// V0
4// IDEA: 2-Pass DFS (Boundary Elimination)
5/**
6 * Algorithm:
7 * Pass 1: Start from all boundary cells and flood-fill to eliminate
8 *         all islands connected to the boundary (these cannot be closed)
9 * Pass 2: Count remaining land cells as closed islands
10 *
11 * Time: O(m×n), Space: O(m×n) for recursion stack
12 */
13public int closedIsland(int[][] grid) {
14    if (grid == null || grid.length == 0) {
15        return 0;
16    }
17
18    int rows = grid.length;
19    int cols = grid[0].length;
20
21    // Pass 1: Eliminate boundary-connected islands
22    // Flood top and bottom borders
23    for (int c = 0; c < cols; c++) {
24        flood(grid, 0, c);           // Top border
25        flood(grid, rows - 1, c);    // Bottom border
26    }
27
28    // Flood left and right borders
29    for (int r = 0; r < rows; r++) {
30        flood(grid, r, 0);           // Left border
31        flood(grid, r, cols - 1);    // Right border
32    }
33
34    // Pass 2: Count closed islands
35    int count = 0;
36    for (int r = 1; r < rows - 1; r++) {
37        for (int c = 1; c < cols - 1; c++) {
38            if (grid[r][c] == 0) {
39                count++;
40                flood(grid, r, c);  // Mark entire island
41            }
42        }
43    }
44
45    return count;
46}
47
48private void flood(int[][] grid, int r, int c) {
49    int rows = grid.length;
50    int cols = grid[0].length;
51
52    // Base case: out of bounds or water
53    if (r < 0 || r >= rows || c < 0 || c >= cols || grid[r][c] == 1) {
54        return;
55    }
56
57    grid[r][c] = 1;  // Mark land as water (visited)
58
59    // Flood 4-directionally
60    flood(grid, r + 1, c);
61    flood(grid, r - 1, c);
62    flood(grid, r, c + 1);
63    flood(grid, r, c - 1);
64}

1# python
2# LC 1254
3def closedIsland(grid):
4    """
5    2-Pass DFS approach
6    """
7    if not grid or not grid[0]:
8        return 0
9
10    rows, cols = len(grid), len(grid[0])
11
12    def flood(r, c):
13        if r < 0 or r >= rows or c < 0 or c >= cols or grid[r][c] == 1:
14            return
15        grid[r][c] = 1
16        flood(r + 1, c)
17        flood(r - 1, c)
18        flood(r, c + 1)
19        flood(r, c - 1)
20
21    # Pass 1: Eliminate boundary islands
22    for c in range(cols):
23        flood(0, c)
24        flood(rows - 1, c)
25
26    for r in range(rows):
27        flood(r, 0)
28        flood(r, cols - 1)
29
30    # Pass 2: Count closed islands
31    count = 0
32    for r in range(1, rows - 1):
33        for c in range(1, cols - 1):
34            if grid[r][c] == 0:
35                count += 1
36                flood(r, c)
37
38    return count

2-13) Pacific Atlantic Water Flow

1// java
2// LC 417
3// V0
4// IDEA : DFS (fixed by GPT)
5
6public List<List<Integer>> pacificAtlantic(int[][] heights) {
7
8    if (heights == null || heights.length == 0 || heights[0].length == 0) {
9        return new ArrayList<>();
10    }
11
12    int l = heights.length;
13    int w = heights[0].length;
14
15    /**
16     *
17     * The pacificReachable and atlanticReachable arrays are used to keep track
18     * of which cells in the matrix can reach the Pacific and Atlantic oceans, respectively.
19     *
20     *
21     * - pacificReachable[i][j] will be true if water
22     *   can flow from cell (i, j) to the Pacific Ocean.
23     *   The Pacific Ocean is on the top and left edges of the matrix.
24     *
25     * - atlanticReachable[i][j] will be true if water
26     *   can flow from cell (i, j) to the Atlantic Ocean.
27     *   The Atlantic Ocean is on the bottom and right edges of the matrix.
28     *
29     *
30     * NOTE !!!!
31     *
32     * The pacificReachable and atlanticReachable arrays serve a dual purpose:
33     *
34     * Tracking Reachability: They track whether each cell can reach the respective ocean.
35     *
36     * Tracking Visited Cells: They also help in tracking whether a cell has already
37     *                         been visited during the depth-first search (DFS)
38     *                         to prevent redundant work and infinite loops.
39     *
40     *
41     *   NOTE !!!
42     *
43     *    we use `boolean[][]` to track if a cell is reachable
44     */
45    boolean[][] pacificReachable = new boolean[l][w];
46    boolean[][] atlanticReachable = new boolean[l][w];
47
48    // check on x-axis
49    /**
50     *  NOTE !!!
51     *
52     *   we loop EVERY `cell` at x-axis  ( (x_1, 0), (x_2, 0), .... (x_1, l - 1), (x_2, l - 1) ... )
53     *
54     */
55    for (int x = 0; x < w; x++) {
56        dfs(heights, pacificReachable, 0, x);
57        dfs(heights, atlanticReachable, l - 1, x);
58    }
59
60    // check on y-axis
61    /**
62     *  NOTE !!!
63     *
64     *   we loop EVERY `cell` at y-axis  (  (0, y_1), (0, y_2), .... (w-1, y_1), (w-1, y_2), ... )
65     *
66     */
67    for (int y = 0; y < l; y++) {
68        dfs(heights, pacificReachable, y, 0);
69        dfs(heights, atlanticReachable, y, w - 1);
70    }
71
72    List<List<Integer>> commonCells = new ArrayList<>();
73    for (int i = 0; i < l; i++) {
74        for (int j = 0; j < w; j++) {
75            if (pacificReachable[i][j] && atlanticReachable[i][j]) {
76                commonCells.add(Arrays.asList(i, j));
77            }
78        }
79    }
80    return commonCells;
81}
82
83/**
84 *  NOTE !!!
85 *
86 *   this dfs func return NOTHING,
87 *   e.g. it updates the matrix value `in place`
88 *
89 *   example:  we pass `pacificReachable` as param to dfs,
90 *             it modifies values in pacificReachable in place,
91 *             but NOT return pacificReachable as response
92 */
93private void dfs(int[][] heights, boolean[][] reachable, int y, int x) {
94
95    int l = heights.length;
96    int w = heights[0].length;
97
98    reachable[y][x] = true;
99
100    int[][] directions = new int[][]{{0, 1}, {1, 0}, {-1, 0}, {0, -1}};
101    for (int[] dir : directions) {
102        int newY = y + dir[0];
103        int newX = x + dir[1];
104
105        /**
106         *  NOTE !!!  only meet below conditions, then do recursion call
107         *
108         *  1. newX, newY still in range
109         *  2. newX, newY is still not reachable (!reachable[newY][newX])
110         *  3. heights[newY][newX] >= heights[y][x]
111         *
112         *
113         *  NOTE !!!
114         *
115         *  The condition !reachable[newY][newX] in the dfs function
116         *  ensures that each cell is only processed once
117         *
118         *  1. Avoid Infinite Loops
119         *  2. Efficiency
120         *  3. Correctness
121         *
122         *
123         *  NOTE !!! "inverse" comparison
124         *
125         *  we use the "inverse" comparison, e.g.  heights[newY][newX] >= heights[y][x]
126         *  so we start from "cur point" (heights[y][x]), and compare with "next point" (heights[newY][newX])
127         *  if "next point" is "higher" than "cur point"  (e.g. heights[newY][newX] >= heights[y][x])
128         *  -> then means water at "next point" can flow to "cur point"
129         *  -> then we keep track back to next point of then "next point"
130         *  -> repeat ...
131         */
132        if (newY >= 0 && newY < l && newX >= 0 && newX < w && !reachable[newY][newX] && heights[newY][newX] >= heights[y][x]) {
133            dfs(heights, reachable, newY, newX);
134        }
135    }
136}

2-12) Minesweeper

1// java
2// LC 529
3
4// (there is also BFS solution)
5
6// V1
7// IDEA: DFS + ARRAY OP (GPT)
8public char[][] updateBoard_1(char[][] board, int[] click) {
9    int rows = board.length;
10    int cols = board[0].length;
11
12    int x = click[0], y = click[1];
13
14    // Edge case: 1x1 grid
15    if (rows == 1 && cols == 1) {
16        if (board[0][0] == 'M') {
17            board[0][0] = 'X';
18        } else {
19            board[0][0] = 'B'; // Fix: properly set 'B' if it's 'E'
20        }
21        return board;
22    }
23
24    // If a mine is clicked, mark as 'X'
25    if (board[x][y] == 'M') {
26        board[x][y] = 'X';
27        return board;
28    }
29
30    // Otherwise, reveal cells recursively
31    reveal_1(board, x, y);
32    return board;
33}
34
35private void reveal_1(char[][] board, int x, int y) {
36    int rows = board.length;
37    int cols = board[0].length;
38
39// Boundary check and already revealed check
40/** NOTE !!!
41 *
42 *  - 1) 'E' represents an unrevealed empty square,
43 *
44 *  - 2) board[x][y] != 'E'
45 *      -> ensures that we only process unrevealed empty cells ('E')
46 *         and avoid unnecessary recursion.
47 *
48 *   - 3) board[x][y] != 'E'
49 *   •  Avoids re-processing non-‘E’ cells
50 *   •  The board can have:
51 *      •   'M' → Mine (already handled separately)
52 *      •   'X' → Clicked mine (game over case)
53 *      •   'B' → Blank (already processed)
54 *      •   '1' to '8' → Number (already processed)
55 *  •   If a cell is not 'E', it means:
56 *      •   It has already been processed
57 *      •   It does not need further expansion
58 *  •   This prevents infinite loops and redundant checks.
59 *
60 *
61 *  - 4) example:
62 *
63 *     input:
64 *          E E E
65 *          E M E
66 *          E E E
67 *
68 *   Click at (0,0)
69 *      1.  We call reveal(board, 0, 0), which:
70 *          •   Counts 1 mine nearby → Updates board[0][0] = '1'
71 *          •   Does NOT recurse further, avoiding unnecessary work.
72 *
73 *      What If We Didn’t Check board[x][y] != 'E'?
74 *          •   It might try to expand into already processed cells, leading to redundant computations or infinite recursion.
75 *
76 */
77if (x < 0 || x >= rows || y < 0 || y >= cols || board[x][y] != 'E') {
78        return;
79    }
80
81    // Directions for 8 neighbors
82    int[][] directions = {
83            { -1, -1 }, { -1, 0 }, { -1, 1 },
84            { 0, -1 }, { 0, 1 },
85            { 1, -1 }, { 1, 0 }, { 1, 1 }
86    };
87
88    // Count adjacent mines
89    int mineCount = 0;
90    for (int[] dir : directions) {
91        int newX = x + dir[0];
92        int newY = y + dir[1];
93        if (newX >= 0 && newX < rows && newY >= 0 && newY < cols && board[newX][newY] == 'M') {
94            mineCount++;
95        }
96    }
97
98    // If there are adjacent mines, show count
99    if (mineCount > 0) {
100        board[x][y] = (char) ('0' + mineCount);
101    } else {
102        // Otherwise, reveal this cell and recurse on neighbors
103        board[x][y] = 'B';
104        for (int[] dir : directions) {
105            reveal_1(board, x + dir[0], y + dir[1]);
106        }
107    }
108}

2-13) K-th Largest Perfect Subtree Size in Binary Tree

1// java
2// LC 3319
3
4// V0-1
5// IDEA: DFS (fixed by gpt)
6//  Time Complexity: O(N log N)
7//  Space Complexity: O(N)
8/**
9*  Objective recap:
10*
11*   We want to:
12*    •   Find all perfect binary subtrees in the given tree.
13*    •   A perfect binary tree is one where:
14*        •   Every node has 0 or 2 children (i.e., full),
15*        •   All leaf nodes are at the `same depth`.
16*    •   Return the k-th largest size among these perfect subtrees.
17*    •   If there are fewer than k perfect subtrees, return -1.
18*
19*/
20// This is a class-level list that stores the sizes of all perfect subtrees we discover during traversal.
21List<Integer> perfectSizes = new ArrayList<>();
22
23public int kthLargestPerfectSubtree_0_1(TreeNode root, int k) {
24    dfs(root);
25    if (perfectSizes.size() < k)
26        return -1;
27
28    Collections.sort(perfectSizes, Collections.reverseOrder());
29    return perfectSizes.get(k - 1);
30}
31
32// Helper class to store information about each subtree
33/**
34*
35* It returns a helper object SubtreeInfo, which contains:
36*    •   height: depth of the subtree rooted at node.
37*    •   size: number of nodes in the subtree.
38*    •   isPerfect: boolean indicating whether this subtree is perfect.
39*
40*/
41private static class SubtreeInfo {
42    int height;
43    int size;
44    boolean isPerfect;
45
46    SubtreeInfo(int height, int size, boolean isPerfect) {
47        this.height = height;
48        this.size = size;
49        this.isPerfect = isPerfect;
50    }
51}
52
53/**
54* Inside dfs():
55*    1.  Base case:
56*        •   If node == null, we return a SubtreeInfo with height 0, size 0, and isPerfect = true.
57*    2.  Recurse on left and right children.
58*    3.  Check if the subtree rooted at this node is perfect:
59*
60*/
61private SubtreeInfo dfs(TreeNode node) {
62    if (node == null) {
63        return new SubtreeInfo(0, 0, true);
64    }
65
66    SubtreeInfo left = dfs(node.left);
67    SubtreeInfo right = dfs(node.right);
68
69/**  NOTE !!!  below logic:
70 *
71 * This ensures:
72 *  •   Both left and right subtrees are perfect.
73 *  •   Their `heights` are the same → leaves are at the `same level`.
74 */
75boolean isPerfect = left.isPerfect && right.isPerfect
76        && (left.height == right.height);
77
78
79    int size = left.size + right.size + 1;
80    int height = Math.max(left.height, right.height) + 1;
81
82    /**
83     *  NOTE !!!
84     *
85     *  If the current subtree is perfect, we record its size:
86     *
87     */
88    if (isPerfect) {
89        perfectSizes.add(size);
90    }
91
92    return new SubtreeInfo(height, size, isPerfect);
93}

Pattern Selection Strategy

1DFS Problem Analysis Flowchart:
2
31. Is it a tree/graph traversal problem?
4   ├── YES → Check structure type
5   │   ├── Tree? → Use Tree Templates (1, 3, 5, 6)
6   │   │   ├── Need specific order? → Template 1 (Traversal)
7   │   │   ├── Need paths? → Template 3 (Path Finding)
8   │   │   ├── Need to modify? → Template 5 (Modification)
9   │   │   └── Need subtree info? → Template 6 (Bottom-up)
10   │   └── Graph? → Use Graph Template (2)
11   │       ├── Has cycles? → Add visited set
12   │       ├── Need all paths? → Track path
13   │       └── Multi-source? → Start from all sources
14   └── NO → Continue to 2
15
162. Is it a combinatorial problem?
17   ├── YES → Use Backtracking Template (4)
18   │   ├── Permutations? → Swap elements
19   │   ├── Combinations? → Start index
20   │   ├── Subsets? → Include/exclude
21   │   └── Constraint satisfaction? → Check validity
22   └── NO → Continue to 3
23
243. Does it require exploring all possibilities?
25   ├── YES → Use DFS with appropriate state tracking
26   │   ├── Grid problem? → 4-directional DFS
27   │   ├── String problem? → Index-based DFS
28   │   └── Decision tree? → Choice-based DFS
29   └── NO → Consider different algorithm
30
314. Special considerations:
32   ├── Need shortest path? → Consider BFS instead
33   ├── Has optimal substructure? → Consider DP
34   └── Need all solutions? → DFS with backtracking

Decision Framework

  1. Identify problem type: Tree, graph, grid, or combinatorial
  2. Choose template: Match problem requirements to template
  3. Handle state: Decide what to track (visited, path, sum)
  4. Optimize: Consider memoization, pruning, early termination

Summary & Quick Reference

Complexity Quick Reference

Pattern Time Complexity Space Complexity Notes
Tree Traversal O(n) O(h) h = height
Graph DFS O(V + E) O(V) V = vertices, E = edges
Grid DFS O(m × n) O(m × n) m×n grid
Backtracking O(b^d) O(d) b = branching, d = depth
Path Finding O(n) O(h) May need O(n) for all paths
Bottom-up O(n) O(h) Single pass with aggregation

Template Quick Reference

Template Best For Avoid When Key Pattern
Tree Traversal Visiting all nodes Need shortest path Pre/in/post order
Graph DFS Connected components Has negative cycles Visited set
Path Finding Root-to-leaf paths Any path works Track & backtrack
Backtracking All combinations Single solution needed Make/unmake choice
Modification Tree editing Read-only required Bottom-up update
Bottom-up Subtree aggregation Simple traversal Post-order return

Common Patterns & Tricks

Pattern: Global Variable for Optimization

1class Solution:
2    def maxPathSum(self, root):
3        self.max_sum = float('-inf')
4        
5        def dfs(node):
6            if not node:
7                return 0
8            left = max(0, dfs(node.left))
9            right = max(0, dfs(node.right))
10            self.max_sum = max(self.max_sum, left + right + node.val)
11            return max(left, right) + node.val
12        
13        dfs(root)
14        return self.max_sum

Pattern: Path Tracking with Backtracking

1def all_paths(root):
2    result = []
3    
4    def dfs(node, path):
5        if not node:
6            return
7        
8        path.append(node.val)  # Make choice
9        
10        if not node.left and not node.right:
11            result.append(path[:])  # Found complete path
12        
13        dfs(node.left, path)
14        dfs(node.right, path)
15        
16        path.pop()  # Unmake choice (backtrack)
17    
18    dfs(root, [])
19    return result

Pattern: Grid DFS with Directions

1def grid_dfs(grid, x, y, visited):
2    if x < 0 or x >= len(grid) or y < 0 or y >= len(grid[0]):
3        return
4    if (x, y) in visited or grid[x][y] == 0:
5        return
6    
7    visited.add((x, y))
8    
9    # 4-directional movement
10    directions = [(0, 1), (1, 0), (0, -1), (-1, 0)]
11    for dx, dy in directions:
12        grid_dfs(grid, x + dx, y + dy, visited)

Problem-Solving Steps

  1. Identify pattern: Tree, graph, backtracking, or path
  2. Choose template: Select appropriate DFS template
  3. Track state: Visited set, path list, or global variable
  4. Handle base cases: Null nodes, boundaries, target found
  5. Test edge cases: Empty input, single node, cycles

Common Mistakes & Tips

🚫 Common Mistakes:

  • Forgetting visited set: Infinite loops in graphs
  • Not backtracking: Incorrect paths in combinatorial problems
  • Wrong traversal order: Using preorder when postorder needed
  • Modifying while traversing: Can break iteration
  • Not handling null: NullPointerException
  • ⚠️ CRITICAL: Not returning immediately when path found: When searching for a path in DFS, must return true immediately when found (see detailed explanation below)

✅ Best Practices:

  • Use visited set for graphs: Prevent cycles
  • Clone paths: path[:] when storing results
  • Check boundaries first: In grid problems
  • Use meaningful names: visited not v
  • Consider iterative: For deep recursion

Interview Tips

  1. Clarify problem type: Tree or graph? Cycles possible?
  2. State approach: “I’ll use DFS because…”
  3. Discuss complexity: Time and space analysis
  4. Handle edge cases: Empty, single element, cycles
  5. Optimize if needed: Memoization, pruning

⚠️ CRITICAL: DFS Early Return Pattern (Path Finding)

Problem: When searching for a path in DFS, what’s the difference between these two approaches?

❌ WRONG Approach: Not Checking Return Value

1private boolean dfsPathVisitor(int node, int destination, Map<Integer, List<Integer>> map, boolean[] visited) {
2    if (node == destination) return true;
3
4    visited[node] = true;
5
6    for (int next : map.get(node)) {
7        if (!visited[next]) {
8            // ❌ WRONG: Ignoring return value - continues searching even after path found!
9            dfsPathVisitor(next, destination, map, visited);
10        }
11    }
12
13    return false;  // Will ALWAYS return false (except for direct hits)
14}

✅ CORRECT Approach: Early Return on Success

1private boolean dfsPathVisitor(int node, int destination, Map<Integer, List<Integer>> map, boolean[] visited) {
2    if (node == destination) return true;
3
4    visited[node] = true;
5
6    for (int next : map.get(node)) {
7        if (!visited[next]) {
8            // ✅ CORRECT: Return immediately when path found!
9            if (dfsPathVisitor(next, destination, map, visited)) {
10                return true;
11            }
12        }
13    }
14
15    return false;  // Only return false if ALL paths explored
16}

📊 Concrete Example: Why Early Return Matters

Test Case:

1Graph: 0 -- 1 -- 2 -- 3
2       |         |
3       4 -------- 5
4
5Adjacency List:
60: [1, 4]
71: [0, 2]
82: [1, 3, 5]
93: [2]
104: [0, 5]
115: [2, 4]
12
13Task: Find path from 0 to 3

Scenario 1: ❌ WRONG (Without Early Return)

Call Stack Trace:

11. dfsPathVisitor(0, 3, ..., visited=[])
2   → visited = [0]
3   → Loop neighbors: [1, 4]
4
5   2. dfsPathVisitor(1, 3, ..., visited=[0])  // First neighbor
6      → visited = [0, 1]
7      → Loop neighbors: [0, 2]  (skip 0, already visited)
8
9      3. dfsPathVisitor(2, 3, ..., visited=[0,1])
10         → visited = [0, 1, 2]
11         → Loop neighbors: [1, 3, 5]  (skip 1)
12
13         4. dfsPathVisitor(3, 3, ..., visited=[0,1,2])
14            → ✅ Found! Returns TRUE
15
16         ← Returns TRUE to level 3
17
18      ← But level 2 IGNORES the return value!
19      ← Continues checking neighbor 5
20
21      5. dfsPathVisitor(5, 3, ..., visited=[0,1,2])
22         → visited = [0, 1, 2, 5]
23         → Loop neighbors: [2, 4]  (both visited)
24         ← Returns FALSE
25
26      ← Level 2 finishes loop, returns FALSE
27
28   ← Level 1 receives FALSE from neighbor 1
29
30   6. dfsPathVisitor(4, 3, ..., visited=[0,1,2,5])  // Second neighbor
31      → visited = [0, 1, 2, 5, 4]
32      → Loop neighbors: [0, 5]  (both visited)
33      ← Returns FALSE
34
35   ← Level 0 finishes loop, returns FALSE
36
37❌ FINAL RESULT: FALSE (Path exists but not detected!)

Why it fails:

  • Found destination at step 4 (returned TRUE)
  • But parent call at step 3 ignored the TRUE result
  • Continued exploring other neighbors unnecessarily
  • Eventually returned FALSE because other paths didn’t reach destination

Scenario 2: ✅ CORRECT (With Early Return)

Call Stack Trace:

11. dfsPathVisitor(0, 3, ..., visited=[])
2   → visited = [0]
3   → Loop neighbors: [1, 4]
4
5   2. dfsPathVisitor(1, 3, ..., visited=[0])  // First neighbor
6      → visited = [0, 1]
7      → Loop neighbors: [0, 2]  (skip 0)
8
9      3. dfsPathVisitor(2, 3, ..., visited=[0,1])
10         → visited = [0, 1, 2]
11         → Loop neighbors: [1, 3, 5]  (skip 1)
12
13         4. dfsPathVisitor(3, 3, ..., visited=[0,1,2])
14            → ✅ Found! Returns TRUE
15
16         ← Returns TRUE to level 3
17
18      ← Level 2 checks: if (TRUE) return true;  ✅
19      ← Returns TRUE immediately (skips remaining neighbors!)
20
21   ← Level 1 checks: if (TRUE) return true;  ✅
22   ← Returns TRUE immediately (skips neighbor 4!)
23
24✅ FINAL RESULT: TRUE (Correct!)

Why it works:

  • Found destination at step 4 (returned TRUE)
  • Parent call at step 3 checked the return value
  • Immediately returned TRUE without exploring other paths
  • Propagated TRUE all the way back to the root

🎯 Key Insights

Aspect ❌ Without Early Return ✅ With Early Return
Correctness ❌ Returns FALSE even when path exists ✅ Returns TRUE when path found
Efficiency Explores ALL paths unnecessarily Stops immediately upon finding path
Time Complexity O(V + E) always (full traversal) O(V + E) worst case, but often much better
Use Case Collecting ALL paths/results Finding ANY path (exists/not exists)

📝 When to Use Each Pattern

Pattern 1: Early Return (Path Existence Check)

1// Use when: "Does path exist?" "Can we reach?" "Is there a route?"
2if (dfs(next)) {
3    return true;  // Found one path - that's enough!
4}

Examples: LC 1971 (Path Exists), LC 797 (All Paths), LC 79 (Word Search)

Pattern 2: Continue Without Return (Collecting All Results)

1// Use when: "Find ALL paths" "Count all solutions" "Collect all combinations"
2dfs(next);  // Don't return early - need to explore all branches

Examples: LC 257 (All Root-to-Leaf Paths), LC 113 (Path Sum II), LC 22 (Generate Parentheses)

🔍 Real Implementation Reference

From LC 1971 - Find if Path Exists in Graph:

1private boolean dfsPathVisitor(int node, int destination,
2                                Map<Integer, List<Integer>> map,
3                                boolean[] visited) {
4    // Base case: destination reached
5    if (node == destination)
6        return true;
7
8    // Mark current node as visited
9    visited[node] = true;
10
11    // Visit neighbors
12    for (int next : map.get(node)) {
13        if (!visited[next]) {
14            // ✅ CRITICAL: Check return value and return immediately if path found
15            if (dfsPathVisitor(next, destination, map, visited)) {
16                return true;
17            }
18
19            // ❌ WRONG PATTERN (commented out):
20            // if (!dfsPathVisitor(next, destination, map, visited)) {
21            //     return false;  // This would fail for graphs with multiple paths!
22            // }
23        }
24    }
25
26    return false;  // Only return false if ALL neighbors failed
27}

Key Rule:

  • Return TRUE eagerly (as soon as found)
  • Return FALSE lazily (only after exhausting all options)

Related Topics

  • BFS: When shortest path needed
  • Dynamic Programming: Overlapping subproblems
  • Backtracking: Subset of DFS for combinations
  • Union Find: Alternative for connectivity
  • Topological Sort: DFS application for dependencies

2-14) Number of Distinct Islands

1// java
2// LC 694
3// V0
4// IDEA: DFS + Path Signatures (Directional Encoding)
5/**
6 * Problem: Count distinct island shapes (translation-invariant)
7 *
8 * Key Insight: Two islands are the same if one can be translated (NOT rotated/reflected) to match the other
9 *
10 * Solution Approach:
11 * 1. For each island, generate a unique "path signature" encoding its shape
12 * 2. Use a HashSet to count distinct signatures
13 * 3. Signature must be translation-invariant but rotation/reflection-sensitive
14 *
15 * Critical Implementation Details:
16 * - Canonical traversal order (always D, U, R, L)
17 * - Starting point normalization (top-left via grid iteration order)
18 * - Backtracking delimiter ('O') to distinguish shapes
19 */
20public int numDistinctIslands(int[][] grid) {
21    if (grid == null || grid.length == 0 || grid[0].length == 0) {
22        return 0;
23    }
24
25    Set<String> uniqueIslandShapes = new HashSet<>();
26    int rows = grid.length;
27    int cols = grid[0].length;
28
29    // Iterate through grid in fixed order (top-to-bottom, left-to-right)
30    // This ensures the starting point for each island is always the top-left-most cell
31    for (int r = 0; r < rows; r++) {
32        for (int c = 0; c < cols; c++) {
33            // Start DFS only on unvisited land cells
34            if (grid[r][c] == 1) {
35                StringBuilder pathSignature = new StringBuilder();
36                // 'S' marks the start position
37                dfs(grid, r, c, pathSignature, 'S');
38
39                if (pathSignature.length() > 0) {
40                    uniqueIslandShapes.add(pathSignature.toString());
41                }
42            }
43        }
44    }
45
46    return uniqueIslandShapes.size();
47}
48
49/**
50 * DFS with directional encoding
51 *
52 * @param grid The grid, modified in-place (cells set to 0 when visited)
53 * @param r Current row
54 * @param c Current column
55 * @param path StringBuilder to build the signature
56 * @param direction Direction taken to arrive at (r, c)
57 */
58private void dfs(int[][] grid, int r, int c, StringBuilder path, char direction) {
59    int rows = grid.length;
60    int cols = grid[0].length;
61
62    // Base Case 1: Out of bounds
63    if (r < 0 || r >= rows || c < 0 || c >= cols) {
64        return;
65    }
66
67    // Base Case 2: Water (0) or already visited land (already marked as 0)
68    if (grid[r][c] == 0) {
69        return;
70    }
71
72    // 1. Mark the current cell as visited by setting it to water (0)
73    // This prevents double counting and replaces the need for a separate visited array
74    grid[r][c] = 0;
75
76    // 2. Record the direction of movement into this cell
77    path.append(direction);
78
79    // 3. Recurse into neighbors in FIXED order (critical for consistency!)
80    // The order must always be the same to ensure identical islands produce identical signatures
81    dfs(grid, r + 1, c, path, 'D');  // Down
82    dfs(grid, r - 1, c, path, 'U');  // Up
83    dfs(grid, r, c + 1, path, 'R');  // Right
84    dfs(grid, r, c - 1, path, 'L');  // Left
85
86    // 4. Crucial Step: Add an "Out" (O) delimiter when returning from this cell
87    // This marks the end of a branch and distinguishes shapes that follow different paths
88    // Without this, different shapes could produce the same signature!
89    path.append('O');
90}

1// V1
2// IDEA: Alternative approach using relative coordinates
3/**
4 * Instead of directional encoding, record relative positions from the starting point
5 */
6public int numDistinctIslands_v2(int[][] grid) {
7    if (grid == null || grid.length == 0) return 0;
8    int rows = grid.length, cols = grid[0].length;
9    boolean[][] seen = new boolean[rows][cols];
10    Set<String> shapes = new HashSet<>();
11
12    for (int r = 0; r < rows; r++) {
13        for (int c = 0; c < cols; c++) {
14            if (!seen[r][c] && grid[r][c] == 1) {
15                StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
16                // Pass starting coordinates (r, c) as origin
17                dfsRelative(grid, seen, r, c, r, c, sb);
18                shapes.add(sb.toString());
19            }
20        }
21    }
22    return shapes.size();
23}
24
25/**
26 * DFS that records relative coordinates from origin (r0, c0)
27 */
28private void dfsRelative(int[][] grid, boolean[][] seen, int r0, int c0, int r, int c, StringBuilder sb) {
29    int rows = grid.length, cols = grid[0].length;
30    if (r < 0 || r >= rows || c < 0 || c >= cols) return;
31    if (seen[r][c] || grid[r][c] != 1) return;
32
33    seen[r][c] = true;
34    // Record relative position: (r - r0, c - c0)
35    // This makes the signature translation-invariant
36    sb.append((r - r0)).append('_').append((c - c0)).append(',');
37
38    // Visit in fixed order (critical!)
39    dfsRelative(grid, seen, r0, c0, r + 1, c, sb);
40    dfsRelative(grid, seen, r0, c0, r - 1, c, sb);
41    dfsRelative(grid, seen, r0, c0, r, c + 1, sb);
42    dfsRelative(grid, seen, r0, c0, r, c - 1, sb);
43}

1# python
2# LC 694
3# V0
4# IDEA: DFS + Path Signatures
5def numDistinctIslands(grid):
6    """
7    Count distinct island shapes using directional path encoding
8
9    Example:
10    Input: grid = [[1,1,0,0,0],
11                   [1,1,0,0,0],
12                   [0,0,0,1,1],
13                   [0,0,0,1,1]]
14    Output: 1
15    Explanation: Both islands have the same shape
16
17    The path signature for both islands would be: "SDROO" or similar
18    """
19    if not grid or not grid[0]:
20        return 0
21
22    rows, cols = len(grid), len(grid[0])
23    unique_shapes = set()
24
25    def dfs(r, c, direction, path):
26        # Out of bounds or water/visited
27        if r < 0 or r >= rows or c < 0 or c >= cols or grid[r][c] != 1:
28            return
29
30        # Mark as visited
31        grid[r][c] = 0
32
33        # Record direction
34        path.append(direction)
35
36        # Visit in fixed order: Down, Up, Right, Left
37        dfs(r + 1, c, 'D', path)
38        dfs(r - 1, c, 'U', path)
39        dfs(r, c + 1, 'R', path)
40        dfs(r, c - 1, 'L', path)
41
42        # Add backtrack delimiter
43        path.append('O')
44
45    # Process each island
46    for r in range(rows):
47        for c in range(cols):
48            if grid[r][c] == 1:
49                path = []
50                dfs(r, c, 'S', path)  # 'S' for start
51                unique_shapes.add(tuple(path))
52
53    return len(unique_shapes)

Why This Works:

  1. Starting Point Normalization

    • Grid iteration is top-to-bottom, left-to-right
    • First land cell encountered is always the top-left-most cell of the island
    • This guarantees the same starting point for identical shapes

  2. Canonical Traversal Order

    • Always check: Down (D), Up (U), Right ®, Left (L) in that order
    • Same shape always produces same sequence of directions

  3. Backtrack Delimiter (‘O’)

    1Example showing why delimiter is needed:
2
3Shape A:  11     Path without 'O': SDRO
4           1     Path with 'O':    SDROO
5
6Shape B:   1     Path without 'O': SDRO (Same as A - WRONG!)
7          11     Path with 'O':    SDORO (Different - CORRECT!)

  4. Translation Invariance

    • Only records directions/relative positions, not absolute coordinates
    • Islands with same shape but different positions → same signature

Time Complexity: O(m × n) where m = rows, n = cols Space Complexity: O(m × n) for recursion stack and HashSet

Java Implementation Notes

1// Java DFS with Stack
2Stack<TreeNode> stack = new Stack<>();
3stack.push(root);
4while (!stack.isEmpty()) {
5    TreeNode node = stack.pop();
6    // Process node
7    if (node.right != null) stack.push(node.right);
8    if (node.left != null) stack.push(node.left);
9}
10
11// Graph DFS with adjacency list
12void dfs(int node, boolean[] visited, List<List<Integer>> adj) {
13    visited[node] = true;
14    for (int neighbor : adj.get(node)) {
15        if (!visited[neighbor]) {
16            dfs(neighbor, visited, adj);
17        }
18    }
19}

Python Implementation Notes

1# Using collections.deque as stack
2from collections import deque
3stack = deque([root])
4while stack:
5    node = stack.pop()  # pop() for stack behavior
6    # Process node
7
8# Graph representation
9graph = defaultdict(list)  # Adjacency list
10visited = set()  # Track visited nodes
11
12# Recursion limit for deep trees
13import sys
14sys.setrecursionlimit(10000)

Quick Decision Tree: Which DFS Pattern to Use?

Decision Flowchart

1START: What are you trying to do with the graph/tree?
2│
3├─ Need to VISIT ALL NODES in specific order?
4│  │
5│  ├─ Yes, in preorder/inorder/postorder → Pattern 1 (Tree Traversal)
6│  │                                        Examples: LC 94, 144, 145
7│  │
8│  └─ Yes, but need to TRY ALL COMBINATIONS → Pattern 4 (Backtracking)
9│                                              Examples: LC 46, 78, 39, 17
10│
11├─ Working with PATHS in tree/graph?
12│  │
13│  ├─ Need specific path with sum/property → Pattern 2 (Path Problems)
14│  │                                          Examples: LC 112, 113, 257
15│  │
16│  └─ Need to AGGREGATE from subtrees → Pattern 6 (Subtree Problems)
17│                                        Examples: LC 508, 652, 236
18│
19├─ Working with GRAPH structure?
20│  │
21│  ├─ Need to find components/islands → Pattern 3 (Graph Traversal)
22│  │                                     Examples: LC 200, 695, 133
23│  │
24│  ├─ Need to eliminate boundary first → Pattern 7 (Boundary Elimination)
25│  │                                      Examples: LC 1254, 130, 417
26│  │
27│  ├─ Need to identify unique shapes → Pattern 8 (Path Signatures)
28│  │                                    Examples: LC 694, 711, 652
29│  │
30│  ├─ Need to validate sub-components → Pattern 9 (DFS with Validation)
31│  │                                     Examples: LC 1905
32│  │
33│  ├─ Need to count edge reversals → Pattern 10 (Bidirectional Direction Tracking)
34│  │                                  Examples: LC 1466
35│  │
36│  └─ Need to count unreachable pairs → Pattern 11 (Component Pair Counting)
37│                                        Examples: LC 2316
38│
39└─ Need to MODIFY tree structure?
40   │
41   └─ Delete/insert/trim nodes → Pattern 5 (Tree Modification)
42                                  Examples: LC 450, 701, 669

Quick Pattern Selection Table

Problem Type Recognition Keywords Template Example Problems
Tree Traversal “traverse”, “visit all”, “serialize” Pattern 1 LC 94, 144, 145, 297
Path Sum/Finding “path sum”, “root to leaf”, “all paths” Pattern 2 LC 112, 113, 257, 124
Islands/Components “islands”, “connected components”, “regions” Pattern 3 LC 200, 695, 133
Combinations/Permutations “all combinations”, “permutations”, “subsets” Pattern 4 LC 46, 78, 39, 17
Tree Modification “delete”, “insert”, “trim”, “convert” Pattern 5 LC 450, 701, 669
Subtree Aggregation “subtree sum”, “duplicate subtrees”, “LCA” Pattern 6 LC 508, 652, 236
Closed Regions “closed islands”, “surrounded regions”, “captured” Pattern 7 LC 1254, 130, 417
Distinct Shapes “distinct islands”, “unique shapes”, “same shape” Pattern 8 LC 694, 711, 652
Sub-component Check “sub-islands”, “subset validation”, “inclusion” Pattern 9 LC 1905
Edge Direction “reorder edges”, “reverse routes”, “orient edges” Pattern 10 LC 1466
Unreachable Pairs “unreachable pairs”, “disconnected nodes” Pattern 11 LC 2316

Recognition Patterns by Keywords

Tree-focused keywords → Patterns 1, 2, 5, 6

  • “traverse”, “preorder”, “inorder”, “postorder”
  • “path sum”, “root to leaf”, “longest path”
  • “delete node”, “insert”, “trim”, “convert BST”
  • “subtree”, “duplicate”, “LCA”

Graph-focused keywords → Patterns 3, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11

  • “islands”, “connected components”, “explore graph”
  • “closed”, “surrounded”, “boundary”, “escape”
  • “distinct”, “unique shapes”, “same structure”
  • “sub-island”, “validate subset”
  • “reorder”, “reverse direction”, “make paths lead to”
  • “unreachable”, “count pairs”

Backtracking keywords → Pattern 4

  • “all combinations”, “permutations”, “subsets”
  • “generate all”, “find all solutions”

Quick Decision Examples

  1. “Find all root-to-leaf paths”

    • Keywords: “root to leaf”, “paths”
    • Decision: Pattern 2 (Path Problems)
    • Template: Path DFS with backtracking

  2. “Count number of closed islands”

    • Keywords: “closed”, “islands”
    • Decision: Pattern 7 (Boundary Elimination)
    • Template: 2-Pass DFS (eliminate boundary first)

  3. “Find number of distinct islands”

    • Keywords: “distinct”, “islands”
    • Decision: Pattern 8 (Path Signatures)
    • Template: DFS with path encoding

  4. “Generate all permutations”

    • Keywords: “all permutations”, “generate”
    • Decision: Pattern 4 (Backtracking)
    • Template: Backtracking with visited tracking

  5. “Find lowest common ancestor”

    • Keywords: “LCA”, “ancestor”
    • Decision: Pattern 6 (Subtree Problems)
    • Template: Bottom-up DFS with result aggregation

Pro Tips for Pattern Selection

  • Two-pass problems: If you need to eliminate something first (boundary, edges), use Pattern 7 or 10
  • Shape comparison: If comparing structures/shapes, use Pattern 8 (Path Signatures)
  • Validation against reference: If checking one structure against another, use Pattern 9
  • Bottom-up aggregation: If answer depends on processing children first, use Pattern 6
  • Try all possibilities: If problem asks for “all” solutions/combinations, use Pattern 4 (Backtracking)
  • Edge directions matter: If working with directed edges in undirected representation, use Pattern 10

Must-Know Problems for Interviews: LC 94, 104, 112, 113, 124, 200, 236, 297, 399, 694 Advanced Problems: LC 124, 297, 329, 472, 652, 694, 711 Path Signature Pattern: LC 694 (Distinct Islands), LC 711 (Distinct Islands II), LC 652 (Find Duplicate Subtrees) Keywords: DFS, depth-first search, recursion, tree traversal, graph traversal, backtracking, path signatures, shape encoding
